President Trump's other inflation headache: beef. 

While everyone is rightly focused on gasoline and oil amid US-Iran war, the meat market isn't giving a respite.

Live cattle wholesale prices in Chicago have reached a new all-time high, surpassing last October's peak. https://t.co/Ny1XzXKLCD

The spike in fertiliser costs is not a food security problem in many nations (and particularly, in developing Asian countries). Instead, it's a fiscal shock problem for the governments subsidizing their consumption.

So far, proving rather difficult for the White House. 

Israeli attacks on Lebanon may derail the whole ceasefire process even before any US-Iran direct talks can start.

COLUMN: No matter what the US and Iran agree over the future of Hormuz, the strait’s status will change. 

But the waterway will never be as critical as it was six weeks ago: Iran's rivals will rush to build more bypass oil pipelines.

U.S./Iran ceasefire reveals oil price pain threshold

Crude oil futures prices have slumped to the lowest for four weeks following agreement between the United States and Iran on a temporary ceasefire and re-opening of the Strait of Hormuz. 

The ceasefire was announced just hours after the U.S. president warned on social media “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again,” sending spot prices soaring. 

The wild gyration underscored the importance of the old adage to pay attention to what people do, not what they say, when assessing the outlook for oil and other commodity markets.  

Hostilities have been suspended even though none of the belligerents has achieved a decisive and unambiguous victory or secured all of its maximalist war aims. 

The messy and ambiguous suspension of fighting suggests the principal belligerents had begun to find the pain intolerable and preferred to try de-escalation. 

So far, the war has been an asymmetric conflict in which the United States and Israel quickly achieved clear air superiority and destroyed a wide range of targets inside Iran.  

But Iran was able to dominate shipping through the Strait of Hormuz and use it to inflict significant costs in terms of higher oil and gas prices. 

The principal burden has been on oil and gas importing countries in Asia, which rely most heavily on supplies from around the Persian Gulf. 

Steep price increases and shortages, especially for refined products such as jet fuel and diesel, have spawned appeals for energy conservation and a rapidly growing web of price controls, export bans and rationing. 

But the impact has started to spread to Europe and even North America, and was expected to worsen significantly if the Strait remained closed through April and into May. 

Prior to the ceasefire, the conflict had escalated to include attacks on an increasing range of hard-to-repair energy targets on both sides of the Gulf, threatening to cause a much longer shortage of fuel and rise in prices. 

Rising oil prices are not the only reason for the ceasefire (and perhaps not even the single most important factor) but they have shaped the course of the conflict. 

Despite the uncompromising rhetoric on both sides of the war, the decision to agree a ceasefire has revealed an important pain threshold for policymakers ...

Spoke with @PnPCBC earlier today about the Iran War "ceasefire", how the rough first day still had plenty of fire on both sides, how flow through Hormuz has yet to actually resume, and what it all means for the supply-starved oil market outlook. https://t.co/4hNisAJWrK

A real ceasefire that even partially reopened the Strait of Hormuz—even under suboptimal conditions and effective Iranian control—would be a godsend to supply-parched markets on the edge of breaking and would spare considerable economic and human misery.

A fake ceasefire announcement meant to placate markets actually worsens the long-term consequences of this crisis by delaying more durable adjustments to ongoing shortages.

The Strait of Hormuz remains closed.

My base case had always been the unilateral TACO, with Iran maintaining some control of the Strait, because I believed the insane market pressure from leaving the Strait closed would be untenable for Trump.

Last night's ceasefire announcement feels like the first step in that direction, but we've still got a ways to walk.

We still don't know what any final agreement will look like, with a huge gap between Iran's 10pt conditions list and the US' 15pt list.

And attacks continued today, even hitting the critical Saudi East-West pipeline.

I still think we avoid the energy market apocalypse because at the end economic cataclysm is not in anyone's interest, even Iran and especially if Tehran can extract concessions and maintain control of the Strait.

Everyone posturing now and we'll need to wait to actually see what both sides are actually willing to concede, not just what they say they made the other side fold on.

"Victory," as it were, will always be in the eye of the beholder, and the only thing about which I'm 100% certain is that at the end of the day both sides will declare absolute victory.

It is the most lucrative time in more than two years to ship Canadian crude to the US Gulf Coast, thanks to the effective closing of the Strait of Hormuz.

Canadian heavy oil for June delivery traded at a $13.55 premium on the US Gulf Coast to the May price in Alberta as of end of day Tuesday, the widest since December of 2023, according to Link Data Services and General Index prices. The spread is the largest since before the expanded Trans Mountain pipeline to the Pacific Coast began operation, alleviating a persistent export bottleneck that caused large price discounts in Alberta.

This sprawling petrochemical complex is currently flaring an immense amount of natural gas (which originates from the world's largest gas field) because it can't process it into other products. This could potentially withhold a couple of hundred thousand barrels per day in exports; but only of refined products/gas condensate; and not crude oil.

#Commodities:

#Crudeoil prices trade 15% lower, well below USD 100, after the US and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire, with Tehran reopening the Strait of Hormuz conditional on no further attacks from US or Israeli forces. While significant uncertainty remains, the reopening may mark the beginning of a de-escalation following a conflict that severely disrupted supplies of crude, refined fuels, fertilizers and key industrial metals. More than 800 vessels remain stranded in the Persian Gulf, and shipowners are likely to seek firm security assurances before both exiting and, importantly, re-entering with empty vessels for loading.

Despite the easing in headline prices, near-term supply tightness persists. Delivery timelines—from loading to final discharge—mean that prompt availability will likely remain constrained for several weeks. Note Dated Brent traded at USD 144 per barrel on Tuesday. Watch prompt futures spreads, which have roughly halved from recent extremes, for any further signs of normalisation. 

Across metals, prices moved higher as the dollar softened and bond yields declined. Precious metals, led by gold and silver, found support in expectations that easing inflation pressures could reopen the path for rate cuts. Industrial metals also advanced, with lower energy prices helping to alleviate recession concerns and improve the demand outlook.
